key_concerns: Multiple statistical errors in original briefing document, including misstated confidence intervals and risk differences
Inconsistent data cut-off dates for safety analyses undermine reliability
Potential over-attribution of fractures with broad search criteria, narrowing changes incidence
Unclear attribution of hepatic event with dechallenge/rechallenge timing
Corrected medical encounters show higher healthcare utilization with imetelstat
key_positives: Corrected placebo CI for RBC-TI suggests lower placebo response, potentially favoring treatment effect
Narrow fracture definition reduces imetelstat-associated fracture rate from 8% to 3%
Hepatic adjudication committee did not attribute the event to imetelstat and no confirmed Hy's law cases

evidence_notes: Correction of RBC-TI placebo CI from (12.6, 34.2) to (0.4, 11.5) significantly alters the efficacy context
Grade 3-4 neutrophil decrease risk difference corrected from +6.4 to +64, indicating much higher neutropenia risk
Medical encounters per patient for imetelstat corrected to 1.3 vs placebo 1.6, still higher but with correct numbers
Fracture rates using narrow definition: 3% overall, 4% at ≥7.1 mg/kg, versus broad 8%
Hepatic event: no recurrence of Hy's law criteria lab abnormalities upon re-challenge with deferasirox, but timing attribution difficulty remains

Potential over-attribution of fractures with broad search criteria, narrowing changes incidence

Corrected medical encounters show higher healthcare utilization with imetelstat

Narrow fracture definition reduces imetelstat-associated fracture rate from 8% to 3%
Hepatic adjudication committee did not attribute the event to imetelstat and no confirmed Hy's law cases

questions_framing.inferred_polarity: likely skeptical due to significant data corrections and safety concerns

document.title: March 14, 2024 Meeting of the Oncologic Drugs Advisory Committee- FDA Briefing Document- Errata

signal.label: MIXED
rationale: Concerns: Multiple statistical errors in original briefing document, including misstated confidence intervals and risk differences; Inconsistent data cut-off dates for safety analyses undermine reliability; Potential over-attribution of fractures with broad search criteria, narrowing changes incidence; Unclear attribution of hepatic event with dechallenge/rechallenge timing; Corrected medical encounters show higher healthcare utilization with imetelstat

Positives: Corrected placebo CI for RBC-TI suggests lower placebo response, potentially favoring treatment effect; Narrow fracture definition reduces imetelstat-associated fracture rate from 8% to 3%; Hepatic adjudication committee did not attribute the event to imetelstat and no confirmed Hy's law cases
